original_abstract: The analysis of the so-called Decommunization Act’s application, after several years of its being in force, points to a significant legal problem. The recent decisions of the Supreme Administrative Court further expose said problem. The issue can be encompassed in the following question: what is, ultimately, the scope of voivodes’ duties with regard to investigating the facts of decommunization proceedings, especially in view of the opinions drawn up by the Institute of National Remembrance (Instytut Pamięci Narodowej, IPN) for the purposes of these proceedings? The answer to this question depends largely on the weight given to these opinions. In particular, if the voivodes are not bound by the IPN’s decisions, one may argue that they should be required to investigate the factual background on the issues on which the IPN had already fi led an opinion. Consequently, voivodes should make historical and legal decisions against the analyzed factual background independently.Analyses included in the current study suggest that, considering the recent decisions of the Supreme Administrative Court of Poland (which have a significant impact on the legal practice), voivodes are not bound by the IPN’s opinions. There is a strong emphasis on the view that the IPN’s opinions should each time be treated as part of evidence subject to verification, constituting grounds for proceedings pursuant to the provisions on the Decommunization Act. Therefore, in each case, verifying those opinions should be the obligation of the voivode. At the conceptual level, there was a condition that voivodes’ verifications of opinions should not interfere in substantive issues and should only examine methodological issues. At the practical level, however, there are cases in which verifying the IPN’s opinions beyond the methodological scope is allowed. The findings indicated above are considered in the context of the author’s belief that: — the analysis of the text of the Decommunization Act must lead to the conclusion that the legislator decided on the binding force of the IPN’s opinions, especially with regards to voivodes; — considering the legal status of the IPN, the scope of its tasks and competences, and the scope of tasks, capacity and competences of voivodes — as well as the specific nature of activities and proceedings based on the Decommunization Act — the opinions of the IPN cannot be treated as a regular part of evidence, requiring verification by the voivodes; from a systemic perspective, they should be treated as statements of knowledge by a competent state institution cooperating with the voivodes in activities and proceedings related to the Decommunization Act.
humanized_abstract: The purpose of this paper is to present a summary of the analysis of the application of the so-called Act on Decomunization carried out after several years of its entry into force, as well as the Supreme Administrative Court’s judgements presented in this regard. The main problem underlying the subject matter of the article can be reduced to the question of what range of tasks should be implemented by the voivodes when investigating the facts that should serve as the basis for conducting proceedings under the Act on Decomunization. This question should be clarified, taking into account the opinions of the Institute of National Remembrance (Instytut Pamięci Narodowej, IPN) issued in relation to these proceedings. As it has been shown in the analyses published in this paper, the courts’ judgements, which — in line with the views on the matter introduced by the Supreme Administrative Court — have a significant impact on the practice of law in relation to decomunization proceedings, suggest that the voivodes are not bound by the IPN’s opinions. One of the assumptions of this paper is that the IPN’s opinions should be treated as part of the evidence that needs to be verified, and which — under the Act on Decomunization — constitutes a basis for conducting proceedings in each case. In other words, it should be verified every time, at the request of the voivode. This approach was presented with a condition that — conceptually speaking — the verification of the IPN’s opinions by the voivodes should not refer to any aspects of the substance of these proceedings, but only to their methodological aspects. In practice, however, it has been shown in the analyses included in this publication that verification of the IPN’s opinions that go beyond methodological issues is possible. The conclusions contained in this paper are presented against the backdrop of the author’s belief in the following: — a verification of the IPN’s opinions contained in this Act requires an analysis of the wording of the Act itself, which leads to the conclusion that the author of the Act assumed the IPN’s opinions to be binding, including in relation to the activities implemented by the voivodes; — the IPNIP’s opinions should not be treated as ordinary part of the evidence, therefore they do not require verification of their correctness by the voivodes; an interpretation of the IPN’s opinions as statements of knowledge by a state authority cooperating with the voivodes in the implementation of activities and proceedings under the Act on Decomunization appears to be most fitting from a systemic perspective.
